BSM24105 · Ravensbourne University London

Welcome to my
Employability Skills
Portfolio

My name is Saraswati Pariyar and I am a BSc (Hons) Business Management with International Year I student. This portfolio documents how I have developed five employability skills across the Spring 2026 Operations and Project Management module, with evidence drawn from in-class activities, the RainyDay Café Business Project Plan (Task 1) and the consultant-style Project Presentation (Task 2).

Analytical Thinking Project Management Commercial Awareness Communication Teamwork
01
Skill One

Analytical & Problem-Solving Thinking

What this skill means

Analytical and problem-solving thinking is the ability to break a complex situation into its component parts, identify root causes rather than symptoms, and design evidence-based solutions. In an operations and project management context this means turning audit findings, stakeholder feedback and quantitative data into a defensible recommendation.

How I developed this skill

During the module I learned to apply structured analytical frameworks — Goldratt's Theory of Constraints, capacity planning (effective vs actual capacity), and root-cause analysis — to operational problems. The clearest application was the RainyDay Café audit, where I worked through a bundle of symptoms (slow service, walk-aways, stock-outs, customer complaints) and identified the underlying interdependent causes.

Rather than treating each symptom separately, I used Goldratt's logic to argue that the three root causes — layout dysfunction, staffing gaps, and inventory failure — were interdependent and had to be addressed simultaneously. I quantified the operational cost of inaction using the average transaction value of £6.30 against weekday footfall and conversion data, deriving a defensible estimate of recoverable revenue per peak day.

Evidence
Evidence A
Audit data analysis — 57% walk-away, £1,260 lost revenue per peak day (slide-03)
Evidence B
Root cause diagram showing three interdependent operational failures (slide-05)
Reading that supported this skill
  • Goldratt, E.M. (1984) The Goal: A Process of Ongoing Improvement
  • Slack, Brandon-Jones and Johnston (2022) Operations Management, 10th edn
  • Costi (2022) Creative Thinking Handbook — module reading list
02
Skill Two

Project Management & Planning

What this skill means

Project management is the ability to take a defined objective and plan, schedule, resource, and govern the work needed to deliver it on time and within budget. It includes scope definition, work breakdown, scheduling with dependencies, role allocation, and risk and stakeholder management.

How I developed this skill

Weeks 7 to 9 of the module covered project management theory in depth — initiation, planning, scope, WBS, scheduling and Gantt charts, RACI matrices, and stakeholder management. I applied each of these tools in sequence to build the RainyDay Café Business Project Plan (Task 1).

In particular I learned and applied: the 100% Rule of WBS decomposition (PMI, 2021); Finish-to-Start dependency logic; the role-allocation rules of the RACI matrix (one Accountable per task, at least one Responsible); and Mendelow's (1991) Power-Interest grid for stakeholder analysis. The Week 8 in-class workshop on building a basic WBS, and the Week 9 workshop on Smartsheet scheduling, gave me hands-on experience that fed directly into my Task 1 deliverable.

Evidence
Evidence A
12-week Gantt summary, £5,000 budget table, and SMART objectives table (slide-10)
🖼 Click to upload image
Evidence B
Three-level WBS decomposition: project → 5 phases → 15 work packages (Task 1)

Reading that supported this skill
  • Project Management Institute (2021) PMBOK Guide, 7th edn
  • Kerzner (2022) Project Management: A Systems Approach, 13th edn
  • Fielding (2022) How To Manage Projects — module reading list
03
Skill Three

Commercial & Business Awareness

What this skill means

Commercial awareness is the ability to understand a business in its operating context: how it makes and loses money, who its customers and competitors are, and how operational decisions translate into financial outcomes. For an external consultant role this is essential — without it, recommendations are technically sound but commercially irrelevant.

How I developed this skill

Week 1's session on operations strategy and Hayes & Wheelwright's competitive priorities (cost, quality, flexibility, speed, innovation) gave me the framing I used to explain why operational efficiency matters financially to a small café. The Week 11 RainyDay Café case and the Week 10 Summit Woodwork CEO simulation both required me to translate operational improvements into a financial argument the business owner would actually fund.

In the RainyDay Café budget I had to work within a hard £5,000 constraint, justify every line item against UK 2026 market prices, and present a credible payback case. I priced the POS-inventory module against Square for Restaurants UK and entry-tier alternatives, signage and queue rails against UK shop-fitter quotes, and staff training-release against UK barista hourly rates of approximately £13. The result is a budget that is both within constraint and commercially defensible to a sceptical owner.

Evidence
Evidence A
£5,000 budget breakdown and 4-day payback calculation (slide-10)
🖼 Click to upload image
Evidence B
KPI table linking operational improvements to revenue outcomes (slide-11)
Reading that supported this skill
  • Hayes and Wheelwright (1984) Restoring Our Competitive Edge
  • Heizer, Render and Munson (2023) Operations Management, 14th edn
  • Dobson and Leemann (2011) Creative Project Management — module reading list
04
Skill Four

Communication & Presentation

What this skill means

Communication is the ability to convey complex analysis to different audiences clearly, in writing, in person, and through visuals. Presentation skill is a specific case: structuring an argument so an audience can follow it, designing visuals that reinforce rather than distract from the message, and delivering it with the confidence appropriate to the role being played.

How I developed this skill

Task 2 required me to roleplay as an external consultant pitching the RainyDay Café operational improvement project to the café owner in 10 minutes. This forced me to compress a 2,000-word written project plan into a tight, visual narrative that opened with the cost of inaction, moved through the diagnosis and solution, and closed with a concrete call to action.

I structured the deck around the brief's recommended timing — context (1 min), issues (2 min), solutions (3 min), project framework (3 min), outcomes and call to action (1 min) — and used data-driven headline slides (the £315k annual loss; the 4-day payback; the three-pillar root-cause diagram) so the owner would understand the commercial case before any technical detail. I also designed each slide so the headline alone communicated the key takeaway, in case the owner was only skim-reading.

Evidence
Evidence A
Cover slide establishing consultant tone and framing (slide-01)
Evidence B
Three-pillar operational improvement framework — the central solution slide (slide-06)
Reading that supported this skill
  • Turner and Johnston (2020) Be Less Zombie — module reading list (clarity in business communication)
  • Costi (2022) Creative Thinking Handbook
05
Skill Five

Teamwork & Collaboration

What this skill means

Teamwork is the ability to contribute to a group's goal while respecting and building on the contributions of others. In a project-management context it includes negotiating roles, handling disagreement constructively, and moving the group through Tuckman's stages of team development (1977) from Forming to Performing as efficiently as possible.

How I developed this skill

The module included multiple group-based in-class activities. The most demanding was the Week 10 Summit Woodwork CEO case, which required a group to solve a complex multi-issue operational case in 60 minutes and present the solution. I also took part in: the Week 2 supermarket layout group activity; the Week 4 supply-chain mapping activity (mapping a chocolate-bar end-to-end supply chain); the Week 8 SMART objectives group activity; and the Week 8 WBS workshop.

Across these activities I learned to negotiate role allocation quickly, push back constructively when a group's argument was unclear, and use shared visual artefacts — sticky notes, whiteboards, draft tables — to keep the group aligned under time pressure. The Week 10 Summit Woodwork case in particular was the closest the module came to a real consultant-team experience.

Evidence

250-word Reflection · LO4

Reflecting on my
Employability Development

This module has developed five employability skills that I now recognise as genuinely transferable to a business career. Before the module I understood project management as a set of tools; by the end I understood it as a discipline of thinking — one that connects operational detail to financial outcomes and team behaviour to delivery quality.

The skill I found most challenging to develop was analytical thinking. The RainyDay Café audit required me to move beyond describing symptoms and argue for a specific set of root causes, supported by data. Goldratt's Theory of Constraints gave me the framework to do this, but applying it under time pressure to a real-seeming business problem was harder than understanding it in theory. The discipline of quantifying the cost of inaction — £1,260 per peak day — forced me to connect my analysis to a number the business owner would care about, which is a habit I intend to carry into every professional recommendation I make.

Commercial awareness developed more gradually. It was not until I had to defend every line of a £5,000 budget against real UK market prices that I understood what it means to make a commercially credible argument rather than an academically correct one. That distinction — between being right in theory and being persuasive in practice — is the most important thing I am taking from this module into my career.

≈ 250 words
